docs.title: Home
docs.text:
📐 🎨 🔍 📤 🧪 🐼 
from pycupfm import CupFM
from pycupfm.datasets import load_grunfeld

df = load_grunfeld()  # N=10 firms, T=20 years
model = CupFM(n_factors=1, bandwidth=3, max_iter=25)
results = model.fit(
    y=df['linvest'], X=df[['lmvalue', 'lkstock']],
    panel_id=df['firm'], time_id=df['year'],
    var_names=['lmvalue', 'lkstock'], dep_var='linvest'
)
results.summary()
============================================================================== cupfm — Panel Cointegration with Common Factors v1.0.0 Bai, Kao & Ng (2009, JoE 149:82-99) | Bai & Kao (2005, SSRN-1815227) ============================================================================== Panel Information -------------------------------------------------------------------------- Dependent variable : linvest Regressors : lmvalue, lkstock Cross-sections (N) : 10 Time periods (T) : 20 Observations (N×T) : 200 Panel type : Balanced Common factors (r) : 1 Bandwidth (M) : 3 (bartlett) Max iterations : 25 CupFM iterations : 25 -------------------------------------------------------------------------- Estimation Results -------------------------------------------------------------------------- Variable | LSDV Bai FM CupFM CupFM-bar CupBC --------------+---------------------------------------------------------- lmvalue | 0.5224*** 0.5840*** 0.7421*** 0.6952*** 0.5824*** | ( 6.81) ( 11.41) ( 10.43) ( 7.58) ( 11.38) --------------+---------------------------------------------------------- lkstock | 0.0827*** 0.0789*** 0.0664** 0.0716* 0.0791*** | ( 3.35) ( 2.92) ( 2.34) ( 1.87) ( 2.93) -------------------------------------------------------------------------- t-statistics in parentheses | *** p<0.01 ** p<0.05 * p<0.10 CupFM = recommended (BKN 2009, Theorem 3) | CupFM-bar = Z-bar variant 📊 Coefficient Comparison — All 5 estimators with 95% confidence intervals 📈 Estimated Common Factors — F̂ₜ time series with r = 1 📉 Factor Loadings — Heterogeneous λᵢ across cross-sections 🔄 CupFM Convergence — β estimates across iterations 🌡️ Long-Run Covariance Ω̂ — Kernel-estimated covariance matrix ⭐ Bai-Ng IC — Automatic factor number selection (r* = optimal)

Panel cointegrating regression with common factor structure:

\[y_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta' x_{it} + \lambda_i' F_t + u_{it}\]

where \(F_t\) are \(r\) common I(1) stochastic trends and \(\lambda_i\) are heterogeneous loadings.

EstimatorMethodIteratesReferenceStatus LSDVWithin / FE✗Biased baseline⚠️ Biased Bai FMFM correction (1-step)✗Bai & Kao (2005)✅ Consistent CupFM ★FM + continuous updating✓BKN (2009) Thm 3⭐ Recommended CupFM-barFM + Z-bar instrument✓BKN (2009)✅ Consistent CupBCBC + updating✓BKN (2009) Thm 2✅ Consistent 
ssc install cupfm
cupfm y x1 x2, nfactors(2) bandwidth(5) plot

docs.title: 🔬 PyCupFM
docs.text: Panel Cointegration with Common Factors Python implementation of all 5 estimators from Bai, Kao & Ng (2009, Journal of Econometrics) and Bai & Kao (2005)

docs.title: 📦 Install in one line
docs.text: pip install pycupfm

docs.title: 5 Estimators
docs.text: LSDV, Bai FM, CupFM ★, CupFM-bar, CupBC — faithfully translated from the original GAUSS source code

docs.title: Beautiful Visualizations
docs.text: 9 publication-quality plot types with premium academic aesthetics using matplotlib

docs.title: Auto Factor Selection
docs.text: Bai & Ng (2002) IC for automatic factor number selection + auto-bandwidth

docs.title: Export Everywhere
docs.text: LaTeX, Excel, CSV, HTML tables — ready for your next paper submission

docs.title: Monte Carlo Tools
docs.text: Built-in DGP simulation replicating BKN (2009) Tables 1-4

docs.title: Pandas Native
docs.text: DataFrame input/output with automatic variable name inference

docs.title: 👤 Dr. Merwan Roudane
docs.text:
📧 merwanroudane920@gmail.com

🔗 GitHub · PyPI · Stata SSC

docs.title: CupFM
docs.text:
from pycupfm import CupFM

model = CupFM(
    n_factors=2,          # int or 'auto'
    kernel='bartlett',    # 'bartlett', 'parzen', 'qs'
    bandwidth=5,          # int or 'auto'
    max_iter=20,          # int (1-500)
    tol=1e-4,             # float
    auto_rmax=8,          # int (max r for auto-selection)
    verbose=False,        # bool
)

docs.title: model.fit(y, X, panel_id, time_id, var_names=None, dep_var='y')
docs.text:
Fit all 5 panel cointegration estimators.

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description y array-like (N*T,) Dependent variable X array-like (N*T, k) Independent variables panel_id array-like (N*T,) Panel unit identifiers time_id array-like (N*T,) Time period identifiers var_names list of str Regressor names dep_var str Dependent variable name

Returns: CupFMResults

docs.title: model.summary()
docs.text: Print the summary table from the last fit.

docs.title: model.plot(kind='all', save_prefix=None)
docs.text: Generate plots. kind options: 'all', 'coefficients', 'factors', 'loadings', 'convergence', 'omega', 'ic'.

docs.title: CupFMResults
docs.text: Returned by model.fit().

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text: Attribute Type Description beta ndarray (k,) CupFM coefficients (primary) tstat ndarray (k,) CupFM t-statistics pvalue ndarray (k,) CupFM p-values betas dict All 5 estimator coefficients tstats dict All 5 t-statistics pvalues dict All 5 p-values se dict All 5 standard errors ci dict All 5 confidence intervals F_hat ndarray (T, r) Estimated factors Lambda ndarray (N, r) Estimated loadings N, T, r int Panel dimensions convergence_path ndarray β across iterations

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Description summary() Print publication-quality table to_dataframe() Convert to pandas DataFrame to_latex(caption=None) Generate LaTeX table to_excel(filename) Export to Excel to_csv(filename) Export to CSV

docs.title: simulate_panel(N, T, k, r, beta, ...)
docs.text:
Simulate panel data from the BKN (2009) DGP.

from pycupfm import simulate_panel

sim = simulate_panel(N=20, T=40, k=1, r=2, beta=2.0, seed=42)

docs.title: monte_carlo(n_reps, N, T, ...)
docs.text:
Run a full Monte Carlo experiment.

from pycupfm import monte_carlo

mc = monte_carlo(n_reps=100, N=20, T=40, verbose=True)

docs.title: load_grunfeld()
docs.text:
Load the classic Grunfeld (1958) investment dataset.

from pycupfm.datasets import load_grunfeld
df = load_grunfeld()  # N=10, T=20

docs.title: Plotting Functions
docs.text:
All functions return matplotlib.figure.Figure.

Function Description plot_coefficients(results) Forest plot with 95% CIs plot_factors(results) Common factor time series plot_loadings(results) Loading bar/scatter plot_convergence(results) Iteration path plot_omega_heatmap(results) Ω covariance heatmap plot_factor_ic(ic_values) Bai-Ng IC vs r plot_loadings_heatmap(results) N×r loading heatmap plot_all(results) All plots at once

docs.title: Kernel Functions
docs.text:
from pycupfm import long_run_covariance, auto_bandwidth

Omega, Delta_plus = long_run_covariance(U, bandwidth=5, kernel='bartlett')
bw = auto_bandwidth(residuals, method='nw')

Available kernels: 'bartlett', 'parzen', 'qs' / 'quadratic_spectral'

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial release of PyCupFM
  • All 5 estimators: LSDV, Bai FM, CupFM, CupFM-bar, CupBC
  • Bai & Ng (2002) information criterion for automatic factor selection
  • Three kernel functions: Bartlett, Parzen, Quadratic Spectral
  • Automatic bandwidth selection (Newey-West, Andrews)
  • 9 publication-quality plot types
  • Export to LaTeX, Excel, CSV, HTML
  • Built-in Grunfeld dataset (N=10 firms, T=20 years)
  • Monte Carlo simulation framework (BKN 2009 DGP)
  • Comprehensive Jupyter notebook tutorial
  • Full API documentation
  • Compatible with Stata cupfm v1.0.4 (SSC)

docs.title: Examples & Gallery
docs.text: Complete working examples with actual output from the Grunfeld dataset (N=10, T=20).

docs.title: Example 2: Coefficient Forest Plot
docs.text:
from pycupfm.plotting import plot_coefficients
plot_coefficients(results, figsize=(11, 5))
📊 All 5 estimators compared with 95% confidence intervals — CupFM (green) is recommended

docs.title: Example 3: Estimated Common Factors
docs.text:
from pycupfm.plotting import plot_factors
plot_factors(results, figsize=(11, 5))
📈 Common factor F̂ₜ — captures the unobserved I(1) stochastic trend shared across firms

docs.title: Example 4: Factor Loadings
docs.text:
from pycupfm.plotting import plot_loadings
plot_loadings(results, figsize=(10, 5))
📉 Heterogeneous factor loadings λᵢ — shows how each firm loads on the common factor

docs.title: Example 5: CupFM Convergence Path
docs.text:
from pycupfm.plotting import plot_convergence
plot_convergence(results, figsize=(10, 5))
🔄 Iteration convergence path showing β estimates stabilizing across CupFM iterations

docs.title: Example 6: Long-Run Covariance Ω Heatmap
docs.text:
from pycupfm.plotting import plot_omega_heatmap
plot_omega_heatmap(results, figsize=(7, 6))
🌡️ Kernel-estimated long-run covariance Ω̂ — used for FM bias correction

docs.title: Example 7: Automatic Factor Selection
docs.text:
model = CupFM(n_factors='auto', bandwidth=3, auto_rmax=5)
results = model.fit(y=df['linvest'], X=df[['lmvalue', 'lkstock']],
                    panel_id=df['firm'], time_id=df['year'])
model.plot(kind='ic')
⭐ Bai & Ng (2002) information criterion — star marks the optimal r*

docs.title: Example 8: Monte Carlo Simulation
docs.text:
from pycupfm import simulate_panel, monte_carlo

# Replicate BKN (2009) DGP
mc = monte_carlo(n_reps=100, N=20, T=40, k=1, r=2, beta=2.0, verbose=True)

# Summary
mc.groupby('estimator')['bias'].agg(['mean', 'std']).round(4)

Output:

=== Monte Carlo Results (BKN 2009 DGP) === True β = 2.0, N=20, T=40, r=2, 100 reps Mean_Beta Mean_Bias Std_Bias RMSE LSDV 2.3412 0.3412 0.4521 0.5665 Bai FM 2.0234 0.0234 0.1245 0.1267 CupFM 2.0089 0.0089 0.0987 0.0991 CupFM-bar 2.0156 0.0156 0.1134 0.1145 CupBC 2.0198 0.0198 0.1056 0.1074

docs.title: Example 9: Export Results
docs.text:
LaTeXExcelCSVAll Formats 
latex = results.to_latex(caption='Grunfeld Panel Cointegration')
print(latex)

results.to_excel('cupfm_results.xlsx')

results.to_csv('cupfm_results.csv')

from pycupfm import export_results
export_results(results, 'output', fmt='all')
# Creates: output.csv, output.xlsx, output.tex, output.html

docs.title: Example 10: Different Kernels
docs.text:
for kern in ['bartlett', 'parzen', 'qs']:
    model = CupFM(n_factors=1, bandwidth=5, kernel=kern)
    r = model.fit(y=df['linvest'], X=df[['lmvalue']],
                  panel_id=df['firm'], time_id=df['year'])
    print(f'{kern:20s}: CupFM β = {r.beta[0]:.4f}')
bartlett : CupFM β = 0.7234 parzen : CupFM β = 0.7198 qs : CupFM β = 0.7156

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install pycupfm

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python ≥ 3.9
  • NumPy ≥ 1.21
  • SciPy ≥ 1.7
  • Pandas ≥ 1.3
  • Matplotlib ≥ 3.5
  • Seaborn ≥ 0.12

docs.title: Your First Model
docs.text:
from pycupfm import CupFM
from pycupfm.datasets import load_grunfeld

# 1. Load data
df = load_grunfeld()

# 2. Create model
model = CupFM(
    n_factors=1,        # number of common factors
    bandwidth=3,        # Bartlett kernel bandwidth
    kernel='bartlett',  # kernel type
    max_iter=25,        # max iterations
)

# 3. Fit all 5 estimators
results = model.fit(
    y=df['linvest'],
    X=df[['lmvalue', 'lkstock']],
    panel_id=df['firm'],
    time_id=df['year'],
    var_names=['lmvalue', 'lkstock'],
    dep_var='linvest'
)

# 4. View results
results.summary()

# 5. Generate plots
model.plot(kind='all')

# 6. Export
results.to_latex()
results.to_excel('results.xlsx')

docs.title: Data Format
docs.text:
PyCupFM expects long-format panel data:

panel_id time_id y x1 x2 1 1 5.2 3.1 2.4 1 2 5.8 3.5 2.6 ... ... ... ... ... N T 4.1 2.8 1.9

Requirements

  • Balanced panel: every unit must have exactly T observations
  • I(1) variables: all variables should be integrated of order 1
  • No missing values: remove or impute NaN before fitting

docs.title: Automatic Factor Selection
docs.text:
# Let Bai-Ng (2002) IC choose the number of factors
model = CupFM(n_factors='auto', auto_rmax=8)
results = model.fit(y, X, panel_id, time_id)
print(f'Selected r = {results.r}')

docs.title: Using Your Own Data
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from pycupfm import CupFM

df = pd.read_csv('my_panel_data.csv')

model = CupFM(n_factors=2, bandwidth=5)
results = model.fit(
    y=df['gdp'],
    X=df[['investment', 'exports']],
    panel_id=df['country'],
    time_id=df['year']
)
results.summary()

docs.title: Panel Cointegrating Regression
docs.text:
The model estimated by PyCupFM is:

\[y_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta' x_{it} + e_{it}, \quad i = 1,\ldots,N; \; t = 1,\ldots,T\]

where the error \(e_{it}\) has a common factor structure:

\[e_{it} = \lambda_i' F_t + u_{it}\]

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
• \(y_{it}\): dependent variable (I(1))
  • \(x_{it}\): \(k \times 1\) vector of I(1) regressors with \(x_{it} = x_{i,t-1} + v_{it}\)
  • \(F_t\): \(r \times 1\) vector of common factors — I(1) global stochastic trends
  • \(\lambda_i\): \(r \times 1\) heterogeneous factor loadings
  • \(u_{it}\): idiosyncratic error (stationary, may be serially correlated)
  • \(\alpha_i\): unit-specific fixed effects
  • \(\beta\): \(k \times 1\) homogeneous cointegrating coefficients

docs.title: 1. LSDV (Within / Fixed-Effects)
docs.text:
Standard panel within estimator. Eliminates \(\alpha_i\) by demeaning:

\[\hat\beta_{LSDV} = \left(\sum_i \tilde{X}_i' \tilde{X}_i\right)^{-1} \sum_i \tilde{X}_i' \tilde{y}_i\]

where \(\tilde{X}_i = X_i - \bar{X}_i\). Biased and inconsistent when \(F_t \sim I(1)\).

docs.title: 2. Bai FM (Two-Step Fully Modified)
docs.text:
From Bai & Kao (2005), Equations 7–8. Non-iterative:

  1. Estimate LSDV → get \(\hat\beta_0\), extract factors \(\hat{F}\), \(\hat\Lambda\)
  2. Construct FM correction using Bartlett long-run covariance \(\hat\Omega\)
  3. Apply bias correction once

docs.title: 3. CupFM — Continuously-Updated FM ★ Recommended
docs.text:
From BKN (2009), Theorem 3, Equation 16. Iterates the Bai FM procedure:

\[\hat\beta^{(j)} \to \text{residuals} \to \text{PCA} \to \hat\Omega \to \text{FM correction} \to \hat\beta^{(j+1)}\]

Asymptotic distribution (BKN 2009, Theorem 3):

\[\sqrt{NT}\left(\hat\beta_{CupFM} - \beta\right) \xrightarrow{d} N\left(0, 6\Omega_{uu \cdot x} / \Sigma_{xx}\right)\]

Exhibits the smallest bias in all BKN Monte Carlo experiments.

docs.title: 4. CupFM-bar (Z-bar Variant)
docs.text: Uses the instrument \(\bar{Z}_i = \bar{x}_i - \hat{F}\hat\delta_i\) instead of \(X_i\).

docs.title: 5. CupBC (Continuously-Updated Bias-Corrected)
docs.text:
From BKN (2009), Theorem 2. Iterates plain Cup-LS:

\[\hat\beta_{BC}^{(j)} = \left(\sum_i X_i' M_{\hat{F}} X_i\right)^{-1} \sum_i X_i' M_{\hat{F}} y_i\]

then applies bias correction at convergence.

docs.title: Long-Run Covariance Estimation
docs.text:
The long-run covariance matrix is estimated using kernel methods:

\[\hat\Omega = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{j=-M}^{M} w_j \sum_t z_t z_{t-j}'\]

docs.title: Available Kernels
docs.text: Kernel Weight Function Bartlett \(w_j = 1 - \|j\|/(M+1)\) Parzen Smooth taper (cubic) Quadratic Spectral Andrews (1991)

docs.title: Bandwidth Selection
docs.text:
• Manual: bandwidth=5 (BKN default)
  • Auto (Newey-West): \(M = \lfloor 4(T/100)^{2/9} \rfloor\)
  • Auto (Andrews): AR(1) plug-in rule

docs.title: Factor Selection
docs.text:
The number of factors \(r\) is selected by minimizing the Bai & Ng (2002) information criterion:

\[IC_1(k) = \ln V(k, \hat{F}) + k \cdot \frac{N+T}{NT} \cdot \ln\frac{NT}{N+T}\]

where \(V(k, \hat{F}) = \frac{1}{NT}\sum_i\sum_t (e_{it} - \hat\lambda_i'\hat{F}_t)^2\).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Bai, J., Kao, C. & Ng, S. (2009). Panel cointegration with global stochastic trends. Journal of Econometrics, 149(1), 82-99.
  2. Bai, J. & Kao, C. (2005). On the estimation and inference of a panel cointegration model with cross-sectional dependence. SSRN-1815227.
  3. Bai, J. & Ng, S. (2002). Determining the number of factors in approximate factor models. Econometrica, 70(1), 191-221.
  4. Andrews, D.W.K. (1991). Heteroskedasticity and autocorrelation consistent covariance matrix estimation. Econometrica, 59(3), 817-858.
